The  one common exception is publicly (     publically).

Note that whether the adjective ends in -ie or -ieal, the adverb ends in -ically 4 adverbs and politie(al).

classical), eomie(al), economical), eleetrie(al), historie(al), magie(al) other cases, both forms exist but with a meaning difference. ►    Section 31  for Rhythmic and rhythmical both exist with no difference of meaning. In a few 3 differences        of  meaning grammatical fanatical   medical     surgical     tyrannical cynical     mechanical   radical    typical critical     mathematical physical    topical chemical     logical     musical      tactical biological (and many other adjectives ending in -logical) 2 some     adjectives        ending      in  -/ea/ those ending in -logical.

New adjectives which  come  into the language generally end in -ie, except for tragical).

Some of these words ended in -ieal in older English (e.g. fantastical, majestical, Arithmetical, geometrical and pedagogical also occur.

despotic    geometric    pedantic   tragic catholic    fantastic   pedagogic   systematic athletic    energetic    pathetic    syntactic artistic    emphatic    neurotic     strategic arithmetic   egoistic     majestic     semantic algebraic   dramatic    linguistic    public academic   domestic    strategic    phonetic 1 some     adjectives        normally       ending      in  -/e form is correct in a particular case.

Many adjectives end in -ie or -ieal. There is no general rule to tell you which 339 -/e and   -ieal For hyphens after co-, ex-, etc, ►    342.3.U.

And some can be added to almost any word of the right Certain prefixes and suffixes are productive: they are often used to form 9 productivity vocabulary *341   capital letters but normally (                  the earth, the sun, the moon) the Pole Star    Mars The Super Cinema  the Far East  (Compare He teaches at a university.) the United States The Ritz Hotel  Oxford University the Foreign Office  North Africa  Canada John         Mary      the Smiths b  the names of people, institutions and places, including stars and planets but normally (                  summer,  autumn) Tuesday  September  Christmas Sunday  March    Easter a the names of days, months and public holidays (but not usually seasons) We   use capital (big) letters at the beginning of the following kinds of words: 341 capital     letters JEANS  - HALF  PRICE  (NOT JEAN'S. . .) It is not correct to put apostrophes in normal plurals.
